text: The surgeon uses the umbilical port as the first working port, the suprapubic port as the second working port, and the left iliac fossa port for the camera during a laparoscopic appendectomy in a young male.
The laparoscopic view shows triangulation of the ports, with the camera port positioned away from the area of interest. Visible pus is present in the pouch of Douglas (POD).

The surgeon observes peristalsis of the ureter and adjusts the patient's position to head low and right lateral up to clear the small bowel from the dissection field in the right iliac fossa. Purulent fluid is suctioned from the area, revealing the appendix located below the cecum. Using the working port, the surgeon identifies and pulls the tip of the appendix.

The surgeon manipulates the acutely inflamed appendix, twisting it to better visualize the attachment of the mesoappendix. Monopolar cautery is used to dissect between the appendix and mesoappendix, ensuring the appendix is isolated for easy retrieval through the 10mm camera or working port.

The surgeon carefully identifies the location of the small bowel to ensure it remains clear of the dissection area. Attention is given to the metal portion of the working instrument to prevent conducted energy from affecting surrounding tissues.

The surgeon applies traction to the appendix, stretching it away from the cecum to facilitate dissection of the mesoappendix. The triangulation technique is effectively used to maintain a comfortable working environment, allowing clear access to the appendix and mesoappendix. The appendix is twisted to better visualize the mesoappendix, and short bursts of cautery are used to dissect directly on top of the appendix.

The surgeon performs precise dissection using short bursts of cautery, focusing on small branches of the appendicular artery to minimize bleeding. Traction and counter-traction are adjusted to enhance visibility and control. The metallic portion of the instrument is carefully positioned to avoid unintended contact with surrounding tissues. The base of the cecum and the location of the small bubble are identified during the procedure.

The surgeon carefully dissects the mesoappendix close to the elongated appendix, avoiding the cecum. The base of the appendix is identified where the three tinea converge, with the cecum visible beyond the base.

The surgeon manipulates the bulky mesoappendix to achieve a precise angle for dissection. Small areas of oozing are visible on the mesoappendix. A bipolar instrument is used to minimize thermal spread while dissecting near important structures such as the small bowel and cecum.

The surgeon secures hemostasis and switches to a monopolar cautery, using traction to pull the appendix away from the cecum. Short bursts of cautery open up the tissue planes, with counter traction provided by the cecum in its fixed position in the retroiliac fossa. Thin fibrous strands are visible, and the dissection proceeds meticulously. The assisting hand changes the position of traction to manipulate the appendix, peeling off tissues with the traction.

The surgeon identifies the appendicular artery branch from the iliacolic artery while dissecting the mesoappendix. The small bowel location and the base of the appendix are visible, with the mesoappendix held in place by small branches, surrounded by fat tissue.

The surgeon uses a short burst of cautery with the Maryland dissector, carefully manipulating its curvature to avoid the base of the cecum. The surgeon holds and pulls fibrous strands away from the appendix, coagulating a small vessel from the appendicular artery. The dissection is performed close to the appendix to ensure the specimen remains thin for easy extraction through the tenon port.

The surgeon utilizes cautery to continue dissection towards the base of the appendix, revealing the last branch of the appendicular artery. A bipolar device is used to coagulate a slightly bulky vessel near the cecum to prevent bleeding, ensuring a safe margin.

The surgeon cuts the coagulated vessel, causing the mesoappendix to retract and reveal the widening structure of the cecum. The cecum is held and identified, showing adequate dissection from the base. The surgeon uses an endo loop with chromic catgut absorbable suture to secure the base, ensuring the loop is placed correctly on the appendix side and not on the cecum.

The surgeon places a second chromic catgut loop around the proximal area of the appendix, ensuring it is three to four millimeters away from the first loop to prevent stump appendicitis. The lumen of the appendix is milked to dislodge any fecolith into the distal portion. A third loop is applied distally to prevent spillage from the appendix lumen.

The surgeon performs a bubble walk to rule out Meckel's diverticulum, inspecting the small bowel. The appendicular specimen is cut, revealing inflammatory fluid but no fecolith. The instrument opens to show a stump of approximately four to five millimeters left behind.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to cauterize the mucosa of the appendicular stump. The camera is switched to a 5mm port, and the appendix is retrieved through the 10mm camera port, visibly pulled into the trocar. The 10mm port is closed with absorbable suture using a suture passer. Local anesthesia is infiltrated for a laparoscopic-guided TAP block for post-operative analgesia.
